topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara stared at the blueprint spread across the conference table, the ink lines mocking her with their perfect symmetry. Beside her, Marcus leaned back in his chair, a faint, almost imperceptible smirk playing on his lips as he traced a line on his own identical schematic. A cold knot tightened in Elara's stomach, pulling at her chest. She picked up a pen, her hand trembling slightly, and tapped the corner of her drawing. The sound echoed unnervingly in the quiet room. "Are you sure about this iteration?" Marcus asked, his voice smooth, devoid of any real surprise. Elara felt a flush creep up her neck, a heat she couldn't seem to control. She quickly dropped the pen, the small clatter sounding deafening. "It's... it's the optimal design," she managed, her voice tight. The silence that followed was heavy, thick with unspoken comparison.

[story 2]

I found the folder tucked away in the back of the shared drive, and my breath hitched. Inside were the same wireframes, the same complex algorithms, the exact same iterative sketches I had been pouring my late nights into. Across the room, Liam was sketching furiously in his notebook, his brow furrowed in concentration. When he finally looked up, his eyes met mine, and for a split second, I saw a flicker of something—recognition, perhaps, or maybe just shared, awkward awareness. I quickly shoved the folder back into my bag, the leather digging into my shoulder. I felt my cheeks burn, a hot, prickly sensation spreading across my face. "Interesting approach," Liam commented, his tone carefully neutral, though I could sense a slight edge of challenge in his voice. I just mumbled something incoherent, focusing intently on straightening the papers on my desk, desperate to disappear into the beige walls.

[story 3]

The discovery hit me like a physical blow. I was staring at the prototype renderings, the intricate details mirroring my own meticulous work, only to realize they belonged to someone else. Across the lab, Sarah was reviewing the data, her posture rigid. When she glanced over, her expression shifted—a subtle tightening around the mouth, a quick, dismissive glance that spoke volumes. My jaw clenched so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. I wanted to retreat, to melt into the floor. "This alignment is... remarkably precise," Sarah said, her tone clipped, as if she were commenting on weather rather than a shared, stolen intellectual property. I swallowed hard, my throat suddenly dry, and forced a weak smile. "It required a lot of late nights," I offered, the words tasting like ash.
